title: Reimagining the merchant dashboard from first principles
summary: A ground-up redesign of the merchant home built around jobs, not features. Now the default surface for 800k+ businesses.
constraints: Must serve both first-time and pro users

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: The old dashboard was a launchpad — a wall of links to features. New merchants bounced. Power users built their own workflows outside it. We rebuilt the surface around the five jobs merchants actually did every week.

sections.heading: Problem
sections.body: Analytics showed 43% of new merchants never returned after signing up. Interviews confirmed the reason: the dashboard was optimized for the org chart, not the user. Nothing on the home screen told a merchant what to do next.

sections.heading: Research
sections.body: We shadowed 12 merchants for a week each. Every session ended with the same finding: the merchant needed one number, one alert, and one next step. Everything else was noise pretending to be signal.

sections.heading: Approach
sections.body: The new home is a single column. A headline metric that changes with the merchant's stage. An action queue driven by anomalies and opportunities, not features. A quiet system tray for everything else. Density scales with maturity — pros unlock more, beginners see less.

sections.heading: Outcome
sections.body: Time to first meaningful action fell by more than half. Weekly active merchants rose 18% within a quarter. The pattern — 'headline · queue · tray' — is now the shared vocabulary for every merchant surface we design.

sections.heading: Reflection
sections.body: Dashboards fail when they answer 'what can I do?' instead of 'what should I do?'. Every good product I've worked on has this trait: it has a point of view about the next best action.
